perturbation.original_text: eGain will provide live telephone support to Customer 24-hours-a-day, seven-days-a-week by a trained eGain customer support representative.
perturbation.changed_text: eGain will provide telephone support to Customer during normal business hours.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 24/7 support and limiting it to normal business hours may violate implied warranty of merchantability, especially if the software is critical to the customer's business operations outside those hours. This introduces legal risk related to service availability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Warranty of Merchantability under California Commercial Code
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code Section 2314 establishes the implied warranty of merchantability, which requires goods (in this case, the software and hosting services) to be fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used. Reducing the support availability from 24/7 to only normal business hours could be argued as a breach of this warranty if the software's utility is significantly diminished outside those hours, because businesses often operate outside normal business hours and the 24/7 support was a reasonable expectation. Limiting support restricts the fitness of the good in question.

SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2314. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) Pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) In the case of fungible goods, are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) Are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) Run, within the variations permitted by the agreement, of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) Are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) Conform to the promises or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. (Enacted b
perturbation.justification: The original contract guarantees 24/7 support, while the modified version limits it to "normal business hours." This omission of 24/7 support directly contradicts the implied warranty of merchantability, especially if the software's utility is significantly diminished outside business hours. According to California Commercial Code Section 2314, '(2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (c) Are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used.' Limiting support availability restricts the fitness of the software, especially if it was initially offered with 24/7 support, and impacts its utility, potentially breaching the warranty.

perturbation.original_text: Payment received by eGain after the due date shall be subject to a late fee equal to one and one-half percent (1.5%) per month, or, if less, the maximum amount allowed by applicable law.
perturbation.changed_text: Payment received by eGain after the due date shall be subject to a late fee of five percent (5%) per month.
perturbation.explanation: Increasing the late fee to 5% per month may violate California's usury laws, which restrict the maximum interest rate that can be charged on certain types of debts. Removing the conditional "or, if less, the maximum amount allowed by applicable law" creates a clear contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article XV, Section 1 of the California Constitution limits the interest rate for loans primarily for personal, family, or household purposes to 10% per annum, and for other loans to the higher of 10% per annum or 5% plus the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco's discount rate. A late fee of 5% per month, equivalent to 60% per year, significantly exceeds these limits, and is therefore a violation of California's usury laws, if this hosting agreement is considered a loan or forbearance of money.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Codes Display Text skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Group Code Section Group Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ Add To My Favorites Code Text * California Constitution - CONS ARTICLE XV USURY [SECTION 1- SECTION 1.] ( Article 15 added June 8, 1976, by Prop. 14, clause 35. Res.Ch. 5, 1976. ) SECTION 1. The rate of interest upon the loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, or on accounts after demand, shall be 7 percent per annum but it shall be competent for the parties to any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action to contract in writing for a rate of interest: (1) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, if the money, goods, or things in action are for use primarily for personal, family, or household purposes, at a rate not exceeding 10 percent per annum; provided, however, that any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action the proceeds of which are used primarily for the purchase, construction or improvement of real property shall not be deemed to be a use primarily for personal, family or household purposes; or (2) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action for any use other than specified in paragraph (1), at a rate not exceeding the higher of (a) 10 percent per annum or (b) 5 percent per annum plus the rate prevailing on the 25th day of the month preceding the earlier of (i) the date of execution of the contract to make the loan or forbearance, or (ii) the date of making the loan or forbearance established by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on advances to member banks under Sections 13 and 13a of the Federal Reserve Act as now in effect or hereafter from time to time amended (or if there is no such single determinable rate of advances, the closest counterpart of such rate as shall be designated by the Superintendent of Banks of the State of California unless some other person or agency is delegated such authority by the Legislature). No person, association, copartnership or corporation shall by charging any fee, bonus, commission, discount or other compensation receive from a borrower more than the interest authorized by this section upo

perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the late fee to a fixed 5% per month, removing the original clause's safeguard that limited the fee to the "maximum amount allowed by applicable law." The Law Explanation states that a 5% per month late fee (60% per year) exceeds California's usury limits, as detailed in Article XV, Section 1 of the California Constitution (Scraped Law Snippet 1), which caps interest rates for loans. By removing the clause ensuring compliance with applicable law, the changed text creates a direct contradiction with California's usury laws, as it now mandates a fee that could be illegal.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California exclusive of its conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware could attempt to circumvent mandatory consumer protection laws in California, where the customer (Eliance Corporation) is likely located, creating a potential legal contradiction. For instance, if Eliance is considered a small business, certain California laws provide specific protections that Delaware law might not.
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Consumer Legal Remedies Act (CLRA) (Cal. Civ. Code § 1750 et seq.) prohibits specific unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in transactions intended to result or which result in the sale or lease of goods or services to any consumer. While this agreement is B2B it could be argued that the client Eliance is a consumer if they are small enough (dependent on definition of consumer), and therefore the CLRA would apply. Choosing Delaware law could be seen as an attempt to avoid these consumer protections, particularly if the service is ultimately used by California consumers. This is especially pertinent if the contract contains clauses that would be deemed unconscionable under California law but permissible under Delaware law.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.5. CONSUMERS LEGAL REMEDIES ACT [1750 - 1784] ( Title 1.5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) CHAPTER 1. General Provisions [1750 - 1756] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) 1750. This title may be cited as the Consumers Legal Remedies Act. (Added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces California law with Delaware law. The Perturbation Explanation states that this change could attempt to circumvent mandatory consumer protection laws in California, where the customer is likely located. The Law Explanation also supports this. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines the California Consumer Legal Remedies Act (CLRA), suggesting the changed text contradicts the consumer protections provided under California law by removing the governance of California law.
